questionText: Prove that $x^2 - 6x + 10 > 0$ for all real values of $x$ by completing the square. Then compute the discriminant of $x^2 - 6x + 10$, explain how a negative discriminant relates to your proof, and state the general equivalence '(for $a>0$) the quadratic is positive for all real $x$ $\Leftrightarrow$ it has no real roots'.

steps.description: State the claim and identify the coefficients.
steps.workingLatex: \text{Claim: } \forall x \in \mathbb{R},\ x^2 - 6x + 10 > 0; \qquad a=1,\ b=-6,\ c=10,\ a>0
steps.explanation: Writing the claim with '$\forall x$' reminds us we must cover every real number, not just a few test values. Noting that $a=1>0$ tells us the parabola opens upwards, which will matter for the interpretation.

steps.description: Choose a strategy and give its intuition.
steps.explanation: A square of a real number can never be negative, so if we can express the quadratic as a square plus a positive amount, positivity for all $x$ is immediate. Completing the square is the tool that produces exactly this form.

steps.description: Complete the square on the x-terms.
steps.explanation: Half of the coefficient $-6$ is $-3$, and $(x-3)^2$ expands to $x^2-6x+9$, which overshoots by $9$. Subtracting $9$ corrects for that, keeping the expression equal.
Substituting back and combining the constants collapses the quadratic into a single square plus $1$. This is the decisive form for reading off its sign.

steps.description: Use the non-negativity of a square.
steps.explanation: Any real number squared is at least zero, with equality only when the base is zero. This single fact is the engine of the whole proof.

steps.description: Add the constant to bound the expression below.
steps.explanation: Adding $1$ to something that is at least $0$ gives something at least $1$. The expression can therefore never dip below $1$.
Since the value is always at least $1$, and $1$ is positive, the quadratic is strictly positive everywhere. This proves the claim.
The smallest value occurs when the square is zero, namely at $x=3$, giving a minimum of $1$. Because even the minimum is above zero, the curve never reaches the $x$-axis.

steps.description: Connect positivity to the absence of roots.
steps.workingLatex: x^2-6x+10 = 0 \text{ has no solution, since the value is always } \ge 1
steps.explanation: A real root would be a value of $x$ making the expression zero, but we have shown it is always at least $1$. So the equation has no real roots.
The discriminant is computed directly from the coefficients and independently measures how many real roots exist. Here it comes out negative.

The quadratic formula takes the square root of $\Delta$, which has no real value when $\Delta<0$. A negative discriminant therefore confirms there are no real roots, matching the completing-the-square finding.

steps.description: State the general equivalence for a > 0.
steps.explanation: When $a>0$ the parabola opens upward, so it lies entirely above the axis exactly when it never crosses it, i.e. has no real roots, i.e. $\Delta<0$. The three descriptions are logically equivalent.
Both routes give the same verdict, which is a strong internal consistency check. A positive minimum and a negative discriminant are two faces of the same fact.

steps.workingLatex: x^2-6x+10 = (x-3)^2+1 > 0\ \forall x;\ \Delta=-4<0;\ \text{graph lies wholly above the } x\text{-axis}
steps.explanation: Because $a>0$ and there are no real roots, the parabola sits entirely above the $x$-axis, which is precisely why the expression is always positive. All three viewpoints reinforce one conclusion.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $x^2 - 6x + 10 = (x-3)^2 + 1 \ge 1 > 0$ for all real $x$. The discriminant is $\Delta = 36 - 40 = -4 < 0$, confirming no real roots; since $a=1>0$, the parabola lies entirely above the $x$-axis. In general, for $a>0$, the quadratic is positive for all $x$ $\Leftrightarrow$ it has no real roots $\Leftrightarrow$ $\Delta<0$.
